item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me laugh harder than any -J skip just because of the line "the subject appeared to show no concern over the slaughter of Class D personnel purely for the purposes of a simple experiment." Apart from that the ending gave me goosebumps and overall one of my favourites

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the heads up, and no worries!

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded: Thanks for this, and sorry not to have responded sooner. I really like the link to SCP-3311, and I'm obviously fine with the link to SCP-2139! I've removed the link to the O5 Council dossier - I find those types of generic / hub page links can be distracting, and I think that's the case here at the critical point in the skip's narrative.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Got a few crosslinks to suggest for this one. First off, in another article by psul that features Site-73 (SCP-2139) there is a crosslink to this article. So why not complete the loop and link back there? Additionally, the article's mention of the O5 Council can link to the O5 Dossier. Finally, it would stand to reason that the SCPF might be careful to monitor SCP-3311 in the event that any instances of SCP-2130 appeared inside. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

All instances of SCP-2130 are to be sent to Storage Location 73-E, an automated storage warehouse in the Texas desert. Storage Location 73-E is to be monitored via CCTV. Guard presence should be minimised where possible. Any further testing of SCP-2130 must not be conducted at primary Foundation sites, and requires Level-3 clearance (particularly any tests involving human subjects).

To this:

All instances of SCP-2130 are to be sent to Storage Location 73-E, an automated storage warehouse in the Texas desert. Storage Location 73-E is to be monitored via CCTV. Guard presence should be minimised where possible. Any further testing of SCP-2130 must not be conducted at primary Foundation sites, and requires Level-3 clearance (particularly any tests involving human subjects).

SCP-3311 is to be monitored for any instances of SCP-2130 produced by its effect.

Changing this:

Subjects: Randomly selected Site-73 research assistants

To this:

Subjects: Randomly selected Site-73 research assistants

Changing this:

From: O5 Council

To this:

From: O5 Council

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having just read it today, the O5 line was what made me upvote, so yeah, for me personally, I think toning that down would take the sting out of it a fair bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sounds like a good plan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having been on the site for almost 2 years, I'm considering re-writing this to remove the two things that people have complained about the most:

  • kappa-radiation
  • the reference to the O5s in the note at the end

The effect would be to shorten the draft slightly, and to replace the O5s being affected with the Site Director (and make it the lead researcher that is writing the request to the Site Director).

If you down- or no-voted, would those changes be enough to change your vote? If you upvoted, would the change from the O5 at the end take too much of the sting out of that final line? Let me know, and I can work out whether this is worth updating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I so wanted to upvote until the note at the end. The O5s shouldn't be so susceptible to the anomaly, and moreover, they should be able to recognize if they've been affected. If this thing isn't so insidious that the scientists working on it can isolate themselves from it, there's no way the O5 Council shouldn't be able to. The article reads like an "incompetent Foundation" piece because of that, and I can't stand any serious portrayal of the top brass being buffoons.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I dunno. This can make into something a lot bigger.
Just imagine this scip is everywhere in the world, the kappa radiation is affecting everyone in the world, even the O5s. And only a handful of researchers are aware of this, and they know that it's exactly the reason why the world is turning into crap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Late to the game, but still here!

Problems I have:
- Your k-radiation phleptonium is necessary, but you've attributed an explanatory (or at least semi-ubiquitous) force to other anomalies. That's why people hate Humes, and it causes some pain here. You're kind of supporting a so-so story on the backs of a hundred other skips without naming them.

- I understand the meta angle, but you're performing a critique of traits of the Foundation that are strongly discouraged in current writers. It's pretty iffy to me, because nowadays I flinch at lolFoundation tone, and this is both confirming and taking credit for that kind of behavior.

Positives:
- That stinger O5 line was really, really good. I mean, really good.

Ultimately, I want to upvote just on the O5 line, but I really can't get behind lolFoundation content. Even meta lolFoundation. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wonder how I missed this one. I see what you're getting at, but ehhhhh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, it's a passive aggressive shot at the "throw D class at it" attitude yet well written and believable enough that it works as a main lister(how do you spell that?).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The implied implications got my upvote.

I like implied implications.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This certainly has potential, and some decent creep factor near the end. The main thing holding me back from voting on it is the Kappa Radiation. I get that you're going for phlebotinum, but it's jarring when you look at other cognitohazards and see that they don't mention anything driving the abilities those objects possess. That's what helps makes them unnerving: the foundation can't locate a reliable source of anomalous activity. Getting rid of the K-radiation would help a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This could work if it was rewritten a bit. I don't buy that the Foundation would ever fail to keep count of dead Class-D personnel. Not because they care about them, but because they're sticklers for data.

As for "Kappa radiation": is that the anomaly? If so, it shouldn't have an official name other than "SCP-2130." We should just be told that SCP-2130 is a type of radiation. If, however, "Kappa radiation" is simply a side-effect of SCP-2130, then "Kappa radiation" itself is anomalous to qualify for it's own SCP classification based on your description.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The people who check the furniture find some oddities and think "eh, it doesn't seem like that much of a risk…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, the Batman of Zur-En-Arrh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Figure an O5 is roughly equal to the average head of state of a first world nation. The supplier of office furniture might be vetted and the furniture itself is probably tested for non-anomalous threats, but…how do you test for an anomaly? Or a cognitohazard? Especially one as insidious as this? This raises your tolerance for risk and causes you to take a much more cavalier attitude toward the lives of others. Anyone testing it is likely to not view it as a hazard because, hey, it's just office furniture, how dangerous can it be? And that's where the horror of this article lies. Because it's in things you don't even see during the day. Your desk, your chair, your wastebasket. Things that are only noticed by their absence, but seldom consciously considered. It's exactly the same thing that made Tyler Durden's speech to the cop investigating the fight club in Fight Club so scary, the people the cop was threatening were all the unseen people that surrounded him every day. Same principle here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regarding the whole "would this get as high as 05?" discussion, I just dunno. Knowing how important the 05 members are, and knowing how tricksy these scips can be, I'd have to say from my own personal head canon point of view, nothing makes it into an 05's office/living space etc until it's gone through rigourous testing procedures.

That said, I think it's great that your scip has opened up this discussion..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well you've obviously thought about it, so I can't say fxxrer thxn thxt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If even a comic book character- hardly the most well-known group for extensive planning- I think that there should be some explanation or rewrite of that.

Then again, anyone affected by the SCP would also be less likely to notice or respond to such a red flag, so perhaps SCP-2130 is even more widespread than is indicated.

(OT: Doesn't Batman also have an alternate personality ingrained that takes over if Batman goes rogue or gets mind-controlled?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get what you're trying to convey here, but I'm not really digging it. I know O5 is supposed to be the top brass of the Foundation, but I think that something like that would be what's called a red flag, and in my headcanon (flimsy, but this is just my personal opinion) they'd have some sort of system in place for if an O5 goes rogue. Like Batman does for the entire Justice League.

That said, it's definitely right on the money for tone and structure. I just think it goes too far into the "excessive use of resources", even if it has a frankly terrifying explanation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A classic example of attempting to exit one person's frying pan and going straight into another's fire. An earlier draft had a reference to casualties "not counting Class D casualties", and someone asked why the Foundation wouldn't consider them, considering they are a resource. That gave rise to the footnote, but it has triggered your question.

In writing this, I was not considering a specific test of this SCP against D Class. What I was trying to get at is that there are many casualties of Foundation staff (including D Class) every day. To work out whether SCP-2130 played a role in those casualties requires a level of forensic investigation. Fair enough, if you're looking at x-hundred accidents involving the death or injury to researchers in the past year. But to investigate each of the thousands of D Class deaths, and work out whether a hidden piece of office furniture played a role - too hard.

And of course I'm trying to emphasise the whole "no one cares much about D Class lives" point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This does not count Class D casualties, as it has been deemed too time-consuming to determine the influence of SCP-2130 on such a large sample.

Eh? That makes no sense. The larger the sample size, the more accurate and revealing the results.

Researcher A: Let's throw hordes of class d's at SCP-2130.
Researcher B: Yes. Although I can't think why.
A: Well…. Science stuff.
B: OK I'll get the tests set up
A: Good idea. Not that we'll use the valuable data to glean an insight into this thing. That's going to be WAY too time-consuming. OK send 'em in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well done Meta-skip.

+1

item.title: Re: SCP-2130
item.content:encoded:
Reads the skip

Meh it's all right…

Reads the O5's message

Craps a brick

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I admit, I saw the low rating and was all set for another debacle. By the end of the article, I was pleasantly surprised enough to upvote. The kappa radiation thing was a bit jarring and obvious phlebotinum and really pushed the willing suspension of disbelief, but the addenda really sold it. I really liked how it took the "just throw some D-class at it" attitude to task. Yeah, there are a few valid concerns that others have mentioned, but in my opinion, they aren't bad enough to warrant a downvote, so…upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your writing is solid and you've got the tone down, but ultimately the idea itself is just kind of boring and dull. I'd encourage you to shelve this one and try something else out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip made me laugh a bit, and then become reasonably frightened. Then I upvoted. It's not the best of executions, but it worked well enough for me to enjoy the article once I got to the interesting bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like the D-Class testing is a bit gratiutious, and the article is trying to pull off something like the doubt mirror without the same level of finesse. It's a good idea… but it's been done before, and better. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe the kappa radiation1 needs a footnote?

The change to the final note improves this enough for me. +1

Footnotes
1. An anomalous form of radiation associated with psionic and cognitive objects?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the comments. I have made some tweaks in the hope of winning over no-voters. Have toned down the communique, and tried to clarify that O5 isn't ignoring the issue, just not prioritising it.

Kappa radiation is, of course, "phlebotinum" - a way of explaining a fictional effect that doesn't actually explain it. Necessary here, because the anomalous furniture needs to be detectable, but difficult to isolate within the Foundation. If it's that ubiquitous, I have the problem that a notional Foundational reader doesn't need an explanation of what it is, but it seems real world readers might - I've tried to address that in a way that doesn't break immersion too much.

As for the ending, I agree that the original intention was to imply that O5 is also compromised. However I think it's open to interpret that Site 73 is overreacting - if the reader doesn't see an issue with some of the testing logs for other SCPs, then they may agree that the current risk level is acceptable. As Mister Ious can tell you, the original ending went on to imply that O5 were the source of the furniture, deliberately increasing risky behaviour to advance the Foundation's research - I think we're all glad that got cut.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got that too, but basically saying "Contain and secure things? Nah," would be a little extreme unless prolonged exposure increased the scope of the effect more than is indicated. If it did compromise them that much, other notable effects would be evident and detection would be immediate. It can't be the first time that O5 got turned over due to SCP effects.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the implication is that the O5 are sitting in these chairs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The mention of "kappa radiation" immediately spiked my technobabble sense, though I appreciate the mention of why it went undetected. Others may have better ideas for its identity.

I do question why the source of the objects is still secret, as SCP effects are a major hazard that should be contained with all due haste as per the Foundation's main purpose, even with a "cavalier attitude" toward human life. The underlining and overall tone of the memo are also too unprofessional for a formal request to O5.

That said, the overall idea as an explanation and expansion on certain test or containment procedures is at least interesting, if gimmicky. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had asked the same thing in the forum, but did not receive an answer. I tried to search for it, but no dice.

After careful consideration and re-reading this article several times, I am going to novote. I would very much so like to see this article expanded on, but it brings up too many questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, some explanation should at least be given for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this explains why so many skips seem to forget that animal testing is possible?

Is kappa radiation actually a thing?

item.title: SCP-2130
item.content:encoded:
I've decided to try my first mainlist post in the heavily-deleted 2130 slot. I figure it's worth the risk.

Thanks to Mister Ious for the forum feedback!
